mnemonics.title: CHARACTERISTIC Equation
mnemonics.content: Remember: det(A - λI) = 0. Think 'D-AL I' → Determinant of (A minus Lambda times Identity) equals zero!

mnemonics.title: Trace & Determinant
mnemonics.content: Sum = Trace (like 'T' for Total sum), Product = Det (like 'P' for Product). T and P are neighbors on keyboard!

mnemonics.title: Eigenvector Finding
mnemonics.content: A - λI → Solve (A minus Lambda I) times v equals zero v (null space). Remember: 'ALIGN' → A - λI gives Null space!

mnemonics.title: Diagonalizable Check
mnemonics.content: If geometric multiplicity always equals algebraic multiplicity → Matrix is diagonalizable. Think 'GEOM = ALG' → Good to go!

acronyms.usage: Matrix satisfies its own characteristic equation p(A) = 0
A = PDP⁻¹ where D is diagonal of eigenvalues
AM ≥ GM always holds for each eigenvalue
Extension of eigen decomposition to non-square matrices

visualTips.visualization: Imagine shaking a matrix - eigenvalues tell you how much it stretches/shrinks in each direction, eigenvectors show those special directions that don't rotate
Think of eigenvector as an arrow that when transformed by matrix A, points in exactly the same direction (just longer or shorter)
Just like fingerprints identify people, characteristic polynomial uniquely identifies eigenvalues of a matrix
P transforms to eigenbasis where matrix becomes simple diagonal D; like rotating to align with principal axes

analogies.analogy: Imagine pulling a rubber sheet - the eigenvector is like the direction of pull that stretches without rotating the material
Like a magnifying glass: eigenvector is what you look at, eigenvalue is how much it magnifies (1 = same, 2 = double size, 0.5 = half)
The matrix 'falls on its own sword' - applying its characteristic polynomial to itself gives zero, like a snake eating its tail
Spectral radius is like the maximum magnification factor - tells you how unstable a system can get
